steps.title: Find the two independent clauses
steps.content:
Before the blank: “her work was featured at the Alex Schlesinger Gallery in [location]”—an independent clause with subject (her work) and verb (was featured).

After the blank: “the exhibited photographs, with their vivid pastel colors, overexposed tones, and mirrorlike symmetry, captivated audiences”—another independent clause with subject (the photographs and modifiers) and verb (captivated).

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for joining independent clauses
steps.content:
Both sides are independent clauses (IC + IC). Rule: two ICs with no coordinating conjunction must be joined by a semicolon or period — a comma alone creates a comma splice.
  • "Switzerland, for example;": "...Gallery in Switzerland, for example; the exhibited photographs...captivated audiences." The semicolon correctly joins the two ICs, with the parenthetical "for example" set off by a comma before it. ✓
  • "Switzerland, for example,": "...Gallery in Switzerland, for example, the exhibited photographs..." — two commas around "for example" leave the two ICs connected only by a comma, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • "Switzerland. For example": Splits the sentence into two, but the resulting second sentence begins "For example the exhibited photographs...captivated audiences" without the comma needed after the introductory transition "For example." ✗
  • "Switzerland: for example.": The period after "for example" cuts off mid-sentence, leaving "the exhibited photographs...captivated audiences" as a separate sentence that is no longer correctly joined to the preceding idea. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
